dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: Several times now. I don't know how to handle it.

options: You should confront them aggressively in front of the whole team to embarrass them.
It might be worth speaking to them privately first, or making your contributions more visible before meetings.
Just accept it — office politics are unavoidable and you can't change them.
You should immediately report them to HR without speaking to them first.
Stop sharing your ideas altogether to prevent it from happening again.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: Do you think zoos serve a useful purpose in the modern world?

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: That's a nuanced view. I suppose the key is how well they treat the animals.

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.line: Exactly. A well-run zoo with strong conservation programmes is very different from a poorly managed one.

options: No — zoos are completely unethical and all animals should be released immediately.
It depends on the zoo. Good zoos contribute to conservation and education, but they must prioritise animal welfare.
Yes — all zoos are excellent and every animal is better off in captivity.
Zoos have no educational value and should be replaced entirely by wildlife documentaries.
Animals in zoos live longer and healthier lives than in the wild in all cases.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of starting my own business, but I'm afraid of financial risk.

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.line: That's a very understandable concern. What kind of business are you considering?

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: Something in the creative sector — possibly a design consultancy.

options: That's a terrible idea — creative businesses almost never succeed.
That sector has strong demand, especially digitally. Thorough research and a clear business plan can significantly reduce the financial risk.
You should borrow as much money as possible before you start.
Financial risk is the only reason not to start a business — you should just avoid it.
Starting a business is only sensible if you already have significant savings.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: What do you think is the greatest challenge facing the next generation?

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: That's a sobering thought. Do you think they'll be able to address it in time?

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.line: I genuinely hope so, but it will require unprecedented global cooperation.

options: I think the next generation will have no significant challenges to face.
In my view, climate change is the most critical challenge — it touches nearly every other issue they'll face.
The main challenge is economic — the next generation will be poorer than their parents.
Technology will solve all their problems, so there's no real cause for concern.
The biggest challenge is finding employment in an automated economy.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: I've started meditating daily and it's really changed how I handle stress.

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.line: I've been meaning to try it but never know where to start.

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.line: That's reassuring. I thought you needed a lot of training before it became useful.

options: You need to meditate for at least two hours a day before seeing any benefits.
Just starting with five minutes a day using a guided app is enough to notice a difference.
Meditation only works for people who are already calm by nature.
You should join an expensive meditation retreat before practising on your own.
It takes years of practice before meditation has any noticeable effect.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: I think the education system needs a complete overhaul. It's too focused on memorisation.

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.line: What would you prioritise instead?

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: Critical thinking, creativity, and collaboration — skills that actually matter in life.

options: I disagree completely — memorisation is the most important skill in any profession.
I think you're onto something — the challenge is changing assessment systems, which still reward knowledge recall.
Those skills are impossible to teach in a classroom setting.
The current system works perfectly well and needs no changes.
Critical thinking is a skill that students develop naturally without formal instruction.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: I've been offered a very high-paying job, but the company's values don't align with mine.

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.line: That's a difficult position to be in. What kind of misalignment are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: They have a poor environmental record and I care a lot about sustainability.

options: Take the money — values don't pay the bills.
That's a genuinely difficult trade-off. Only you can decide how much your values matter relative to financial security.
Companies with poor environmental records will always change their ways eventually.
You should take the job and try to change the company from the inside immediately.
Values are personal and shouldn't influence career decisions at all.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: I'm finding it really hard to adapt to living abroad. I miss home constantly.

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.line: How long have you been there?

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: About three months. I thought I'd feel more settled by now.

options: Three months is more than enough — if you're still struggling, you should just go home.
What you're experiencing sounds like culture shock, which is very common. It usually takes six months to a year to fully adjust.
You clearly made a mistake by moving abroad — not everyone is suited to it.
Three months means you've already adapted — the feeling will never go away.
You should stop comparing your new life to home if you want to feel better.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: I've been offered a place at two universities — one is more prestigious, but the other has a much better programme for my specific interest.

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.line: That's a tough choice. Which matters more to you in the long run?

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.line: I keep going back and forth. Prestige seems important for jobs, but I want to actually enjoy my studies.

options: Always choose prestige — the name of your institution is the only thing employers care about.
Research shows that satisfaction with your programme often has more impact on long-term outcomes than institutional prestige alone.
You should choose randomly — both options are equally good.
Neither matters — what you do after graduation is the only thing that counts.
Prestige is irrelevant — only grades and skills matter to employers.
